pages.text: Review The inﬂuence of water on the performance of concrete Kenneth C. Hover ⇑ 302 Hollister Hall, Cornell University, Ithaca, NY 14853, USA article info Article history: Received 8 June 2010 Received in revised form 24 January 2011 Accepted 25 January 2011 Available online 3 March 2011 Keywords: Curing Field control Hydration Permeability Porosity Shrinkage Water content abstract The powerful and pervasive inﬂuence of water on the performance of Portland cement concrete begins with the removal of water from either moist or wet raw cement feed and ends with application of curing water. Heat of hydration is normalized by mass of water. Due to its low density, water content mass in fresh concrete translates to porosity in hardened concrete, affecting both strength and durability. Water- cement ratio is here interpreted in terms of average spacing between adjacent cement particles, and workability, air, shrinkage, and service life are normalized to water content. Mixture proportioning and control of water content in the ﬁeld are addressed. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Contents 1. Introduction Water has a powerful and pervasive inﬂuence on the perfor- mance of Portland cement concrete. Water is driven off in the ce- ment-manufacturing process, followed by chemical changes that make the ﬁnal powder

of hydration. If the inter-particle pore spaces can be fully (or sufﬁ- ciently) ﬁlled with hydration products, however, the performance goal of low porosity can be achieved even though residual, unre- acted cement will unquestionably remain. The fact that water occupies volume in fresh concrete is a great but underappreciated truth. For most practical concrete mixtures the weight of water is less than 10% of the weight of the batched concrete, implying that water is a minor constitu- ent—and if minor, a little more or a little less might not make much difference. But given that the water occupies volume that is the origin of the pore space in the hardened concrete, mix water volume becomes a point of focus. Given that water is far less dense than all of the solid components in a concrete mixture, and that Portland cement is the densest, the 15–18% of concrete batch weight typically represented by cement con- verts to only 10–12% of the concrete volume, while the 8–10% of concrete batch weight represented by water converts to 15– 20% of the concrete volume. Concrete made with paste at water/cement ratio (by mass) of 1.0 has equal masses of water and cement, but a paste with a w/c of 0.33 has equal volumes of water and cement. Thus any mixture with w/c > 0.33 has a ce- ment paste in which the water volume (destined for porosity) will exceed the cement volume. Obviously, a key to controlling the porosity of hardened concrete is to limit the starting porosity of fresh concrete, and that is done by controlling water volume. 5. Water, strength, and permeability Among the consequences of the impact of water content in the fresh concrete on particle spacing and porosity in the hardened concrete, are the well known trends that an increase in w/cm also increases permeability while decreasing compressive strength. This is demonstrated in Fig. 6 based on average (and conservative) 28-days strength data from ACI 211.1 (guide to proportioning mixtures) [10], coupled with a typical range of permeability test results from the US Bureau of Reclamation [11]. Note that varia- tions in the mixture produced wide variations in the measured permeability—a not uncommon observation [12]. Even with the mix-speciﬁc variation in permeability results, curve-ﬁts to all the data sets suggest that permeability increases with between the 3rd and the 8th power of water content! Such relationships are behind the ACI 318 building code [13] requirements that concrete to be subjected to a freezing environment while in a moist condi- tion shall have a maximum w/cm of 0.45 and minimum speciﬁed 28-days strength of 31.0 MPa. Similarly, concrete exposed to deicing salts or seawater shall have a maximum w/cm of 0.40 and minimum speciﬁed 28-days strength of 34.5 MPa. 6. Water and time to corrosion The power of water in inﬂuencing permeability, and thus the rate of saltwater intrusion to reinforcing steel, can be examined via the ‘‘Life-365’’ software available in the public domain, cour- tesy of an industrial consortium [14]. Consider a bridge deck or parking garage planned for a severely corrosive chloride environ- ment, such as seawater or deicing salts. Assume a high perfor- mance mixture that includes 277, 55, and 17 kg per cubic metre of Portland cement, coal ﬂy ash, and silica fume, respec- tively, and allow w/cm to vary to examine the impact of water on the time to initiation of reinforcing steel corrosion. The re- sults are displayed in Fig. 7 in which the left-hand y-axis

but for all of water’s great chemical power (it is known as the ‘‘uni- versal solvent’’) perhaps its greatest liability in concrete is its low density. Compared to other concrete-making materials, a small weight (or mass) of water converts to a large volume. Water repre- sents 10% or less than the weight of concrete, but occupies almost 20% of the concrete volume. When it comes to the paste, the fun- damental binder that holds concrete together, water represents far less than half of the mass, but more than half of the volume. ‘‘Solid concrete’’ is not particularly solid thanks to the porosity originating in the initial volume occupied by mix water. Not only are concrete’s strength and durability dependent on the amount of water in the fresh mixture (and the subsequent pore space in the hardened mass), but shrinkage is also proportional to water content, and workability is exponentially dependent on water. We intentionally increase the porosity of concrete still further with air bubbles to protect the ﬁnal product against the expansion of water when it freezes (and that freezable water occupies space previously vacated by the mix water). Inspectors reject concrete for being too wet, prohibit the addition of water during ﬁnishing operations, but within an hour call for immediate application of water when the concrete surface begins to dry. The contractor responds to these seemingly schizophrenic demands by curing with water, to make sure that the concrete will be durable when subsequently exposed to water. Water thus exerts its inﬂuence on the behaviour of the fresh and hardened concrete to a degree that is perhaps greater than any other variable ingredient of the mixture. And in spite of this realization, the water content of con- crete is notoriously difﬁcult to control. The following paper synthe- sizes much of what is known about water and concrete, presenting and juxtaposing current understanding in a fresh context. 2. Water, cement, and heat The manufacture of cement, batching of concrete, and use of concrete in construction all begin and end with the control of water. Heating raw materials in the rotary kiln is the most energy intensive stage in the manufacture of Portland cement, and the ﬁrst step in this thermal transformation is the removal of water. About 2.3 million kJ of heat are required to vaporize one tonne of water, or 23,000 kJ to dry one tonne of raw feed material for each 1% moisture content. (This is why wet-process cement manufac- turing is more energy-intensive.) A bit further down the kiln heat is consumed in the liberation of carbon dioxide from limestone (calcium carbonate), and further and hotter yet heat is absorbed in the formation of the unstable calcium silicate, calcium alumi- nate, and calcium–aluminium–iron compounds. This pyroprocess- ing requires about 1.7 million kJ per tonne of clinker produced. Given an average thermal efﬁciency of 30–40%, something on the order of 5.2 million kJ per tonne are expended in the production of ordinary Portland cement, not accounting for energy consumed in quarrying or transport [1]. (To put the manufacture of cement in perspective, the production of electrical energy is a similar opera- tion in which fuel is burned to heat water instead of the raw feed for Portland cement, and the energy demand to turn a tonne of water into steam is about 35% greater than the energy demand for a tonne of cement, with both processes running about 30–40% thermal efﬁciency.) The ﬁnal prod

As unstable cement compounds hydrate, the resulting products are chemically stable, (cement reacts with water—hydrated ce- ment does not!) In the process cement compounds move to a low- er energy level, meaning that some of the heat energy stored in the cement in the form of chemical changes is recovered in the hydra- tion process, which to some degree reverses the changes brought about by thermal processing in the kiln. About 1/4 of the heat ab- sorbed in the kiln is returned as the so-called ‘‘heat of hydration.’’ Production of a tonne of cement absorbs somewhere around 1.8 million kJ of heat energy, and hydration of that same tonne of ce- ment can liberate about 0.45 million kJ [1–4]. In freshly cast con- crete heat of hydration that cannot escape to the environment due to the insulating value of the mass of surrounding concrete, or by external insulation, or both, increases the temperature of the concrete. Heat of hydration is normally expressed as energy per unit mass of cement as in the paragraph above. However, since it is the reac- tion between water and cement that causes the hydration, one could legitimately express heat of hydration relative to the mass of the water that triggered the reaction. Thus the 0.45 million kJ liberated by hydration of one tonne of cement can be associated with the approximately 0.42 tonnes of water required to hydrate that cement. The net result is the unconventionally reported ‘‘heat of hydration of water’’ of about 1.1 million kJ per tonne of mix water reacted with Portland cement. (One might express this on a volume basis as 1100 kJ per litre of water when it combines with cement—a poor fuel compared with gasoline at 35,000 kJ per litre when it combines with oxygen!) 3. Where is the water? Liquid water is taken-up by the cement several ways. First, the dry, products of hydration shown most clearly in Fig. 1.3 are pro- duced via the combination of about 4 parts unhydrated cement and about 1 part water by mass. Thus about 250 g of water are chemically combined in these dry, microcrystalline-like structures for each kg cement hydrated. But as can be seen in the ﬁgures above, hydrated cement paste is highly porous with ‘‘capillary’’ voids between the hydrated cement particles, and so called ‘‘gel’’ or ‘‘interlayer’’ pores existing within the hydration products themselves [3,4] (see Fig. 2 ). In addition to the water chemically bound in the hydration products, more water is simultaneously absorbed into the pores and adsorbed onto the expanding surface area as hydration progresses. This water can account for another 180–200 g per kg of cement hydrated, for a grand total of about 420–450 g of water required to fully hydrate 1 kg of cement. The common misinterpretation that cement can ‘‘fully hydrate’’ at a w/c of 0.25 stems from ignoring the equally critical amount of water required to ﬁll the pores as they are created. Water that remains in a sample of hardened cement paste after oven-drying to 105 /C176C is referred to as ‘‘non-evaporable’’ water, and roughly approximates the quantity of water chemically com- bined in hydration products. Water removed by oven-drying is referred to as ‘‘evaporable’’ water, but that imprecise term can be misinterpreted to imply that ‘‘evaporable water’’ is not present after curing in real concrete in the real, and dry, world. But since the drying-oven is a far more severe environment than that expe- rienced by most concrete in-service, much of the so called ‘‘evapor- ab

cement paste. While air content is normally speciﬁed and tested as a volume percentage of the concrete, the real parameter of interest is the air volume as a percentage of the volume of the paste. (Air voids have no signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the freeze–thaw durability of the aggregates.) Rules of thumb that associate required air con- tent with various sizes of coarse aggregate, such as those found in multiple ACI documents [10,13,16,17] (see Table 1 ) are merely using aggregate size as a rough guide to the paste content, since we know that paste content must increase to cover the increasing surface area of smaller aggregates. With the advent of water-reducing admixtures, optimized com- bined aggregate grading, and a variety of cementitious materials, required air content as a function of coarse aggregate size can be misleading. Assume a base requirement for freeze–thaw resistance of a computed spacing factor in the hardened paste of between 0.20 and 0.25 mm, and further assume use of an air entraining admixture that can deliver an air void system with a speciﬁc sur- face of at least 24 mm 2/mm3 (most products can do far better). For this case the standard ‘‘Powers’’ model [18] will conclude that the air content must be between about 11 and 19% of the paste vol- ume for spacing factors of 0.25 and 0.20 mm respectively. Fig. 9 shows the inﬂuence of water content on required air content for a highly freeze–thaw resistant concrete. 9. Water and workability The impact of water on porosity and subsequently on strength and durability cannot generally be observed at the time of adding water to the concrete. Those responsible for adding water to fresh concrete have no immediate visual clue that water addition will degrade hardened concrete properties. On the other hand, the immediate impact of water content on the ﬂuidity of the concrete is immediately obvious. In fact, much of the concrete quality con- trol conundrum world-wide is related to the immediate and obvi- ous workability increase that results from increasing water content. Subsequent negative consequences may or may not devel- op depending on mix proportions, other materials, amount of water added, service loads, and exposure conditions. If such conse- quences ensue, they may not become evident for some time after water addition. When faced with the immediate need to increase the ﬂuidity of the concrete, water is a rapid, effective, and appar- ently inexpensive solution. The impact of water content on the ﬂuidity of concrete (as roughly indicated by slump) is shown in Fig. 10 , modiﬁed from data in ACI 211.1 [10]. The author suggests that the ‘‘basic water demand’’ indicated in Fig. 10 be modiﬁed on the basis of local materials and the mixture-speciﬁc factors shown in Fig. 11 [19] . Examining Fig. 10 , one observes the powerful inﬂuence of aggre- gate grading such that the water demand increases markedly as aggregate size decreases in response to the greater surface area per unit mass. This effect has been analyzed by many, with Day in Australia [20] and Shilstone in the US [21] as energetic proponents of innovative techniques to more accurately account for the size effects of the total aggregate grading. For any given coarse aggregate size designation the water demand curves are increasingly steep as water content increases. The engineer observes a classically exponential relationship between water content and slump, while the practitioner on site observes that the ﬁrst ﬁeld addition o

thing affects everything else.’’ This is particularly true of water as described graphically in Fig. 12 . The ﬁgure shows the combined inﬂuence of slump, aggregate size, and water content seen earlier, along with the impact of water and w/cm on the required mass of cementitious materials. Another ‘‘great truth’’ of concrete mixture proportioning is that lower values of w/cm often translate to higher batch weights of cementitious materials rather than lower water contents. (Once the water content has been determined, weight of cementitious materials is determined by dividing the water weight by the w/cm appropriate for speciﬁed properties.) The inﬂuence of water on shrinkage is also suggested by superimposing the ‘‘PCA average’’ curve from Fig. 8 in both the lower left and upper quad- rants. The upper left quadrant of Fig. 12 allows for adjusting the water requirement by a factor as low as 70% (i.e., a 30% water reduc- tion) or as high as 120% (a 20% increase in water demand). The goal in mixture proportioning is to ﬁnd a combination of water content and w/cm that will produce the speciﬁed engineering properties, produce a workable concrete, and limit total cementitious materials and shrinkage to practical values. The various factors outlined in Fig. 11 are combined to adjust the basic water demand to meet these needs. As is sometimes the case, however, the various con- straints may be incompatible with conventional materials, at which point more sophisticated options may be called for such as self-con- solidating concrete, shrinkage-reducing admixtures, or revisions to the drawings or speciﬁcations. 11. Control of water content Controlling water content is admittedly far easier to talk and write about than to carry out. Nevertheless, controlling the quality of the hardened product for the short- or long-term, as well as the ability to place, consolidate, and ﬁnish the fresh concrete, and to do so with uniformity and predictability depends in large measure on controlling water content. Doing so effectively requires attention to at least those steps brieﬂy outlined in Table 3 (found at end of paper). Depending on the criticality of the concrete in question, tests can be performed to estimate the amount of water in fresh concrete. The microwave-oven drying method (T-318-02) [23], standardized by the American Association for State Highway and Transportation Ofﬁcials (AASHTO) can be helpful in this regard, with values of w/c derived from the test results accurate to about ±0.03 to ±0.05. On special projects in the State of Minnesota (USA) this test method is used. For speciﬁed w/c = 0.40 a bonus is paid to concrete paving contractors when the measured w/c is less than 0.037, and a penalty is exacted when the measured w/c is greater than 0.43. 12. Water and curing The earlier discussion on hydration is at the heart of the curing issue. Since water is an essential component of hydration, and since cement-hydration will only proceed in a water-ﬁlled space, sustaining the hydration process requires that inter-particle voids remain full of water. This can only be accomplished by making li- quid water available to the hydrating cement—it cannot be accom- plished even by sealing the surface to prevent the loss of mix water. One of the most signiﬁcant implications of the mistaken no- tion that 1 kg cement requires only 250 g water for ‘‘full hydra- tion,’’ (thus ignoring gel water as discussed earlier) is a false belief that curing by the applicatio

and that all one must do is to reduce the amount of mix water lost by evaporation. When the w/c is less than about 0.42, concrete that is sealed effectively will dry itself from the inside-out as hydration consumes available water. This is known as ‘‘self desiccation’’ [3]. This means that curing by application of water is especially impor- tant for low w/c, high performance mixtures. But as for many things in life, timing is essential when it comes to curing. The key is to keep the capillary pores full of water for long enough that the desired concrete properties develop. This means that water is needed as soon as hydration starts—making ‘‘curing’’ a multi-step process. Looking back at Fig. 1.2 we see the interlocking hydration products connecting the cement grains. These bonds are virtually nonexistent in the ﬁrst hour or so after mixing, so early application of curing water will separate cement particles, increase porosity and permeability, and decrease strength (at least at the surface to which the water has been applied). As hydration proceeds and the fragile bonds become stronger, the application of curing water can be more robust. Thus a fog-spray or ‘‘misting’’ can be gently applied in the time period between placing and ﬁnishing, and again post-ﬁnishing until the concrete has reached ﬁnal set. At this point more aggressive steps can be taken such as application of saturated burlap or other absorptive coverings. Use of spray-applied curing compounds is also beneﬁcial to bridge the time gap between ﬁnishing the con- crete and application of water as a ﬁnal cure method [24]. 13. Concrete performance in-service It is useful to point out that water is not only essential to the development of concrete properties, but it is likewise essential to almost all of the deteriorative mechanisms responsible for the deg- radation of concrete properties over time. The role of water in freezing-and-thawing damage and corrosion of embedded metals has already been discussed. Water is also required for the transport of aggressive substances that cause alkali-aggregate reaction and the several variants of deleterious sulphate reactions. Abrasion of concrete surfaces is made more severe in the presence of water, and hydraulic structures can be damaged by the cavitation that accompanies high water velocity against a concrete surface. In fact, when concrete has reached the end of its useful service life, it is not at all unusual for the deteriorated concrete to be cut away or re- moved altogether by high pressure jets of water known as ‘‘hy- dro-demolition.’’ While details of these mechanisms are beyond the scope of this paper (see general texts such as [2–4,11,15,25] it is profound, perhaps, to recognize that water is essential to both the development and destruction of concrete. 14. Conclusions The behaviour of concrete is intimately associated with water. Water is needed for hydration but the same water occupies space that creates pores. The pores later ﬁll with water leading to a vari- ety of deterioration mechanisms, and concrete’s resistance to these forms of attack depends on initial water content. Both strength and shrinkage are critical characteristics, and both depend on water. But against all of these reasons for restricting water content, the reality remains that concrete must be made sufﬁciently workable, and there is no more readily available ‘‘ﬂuidiﬁer’’ than water. Effec- tive practices include mixture proportioning that takes into ac-
